beats.title: Introduction to the Kūrma Purāṇa
beats.summary: The chapter begins with the sages of Naimiṣāraṇya asking Sūta Romaharṣaṇa to narrate the Kūrma Purāṇa. Romaharṣaṇa describes the eighteen major Purāṇas and eighteen Upa-Purāṇas, then introduces the Kūrma Purāṇa as the fifteenth, divided into four Saṃhitās: Brāhmī, Bhāgavatī, Saurī, and Vaiṣṇavī. The Brāhmī Saṃhitā is equated with the four Vedas.

beats.title: Origin of the Kurma Purana and the Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of the Kurma Purana, narrated by Vyasa. It recounts the churning of the ocean by devas and asuras, where Vishnu took the form of a tortoise (Kurma) to support Mount Mandara. The goddess Shri (Lakshmi) emerged and was taken by Vishnu. Vishnu explains that Shri is his supreme Maya, the primordial Prakriti, and that all gods including Brahma and Shiva are his manifestations. He then tells the sages about King Indradyumna, who in a previous life saw Vishnu as Kurma, heard the Purana, and attained liberation.

beats.title: Indradyumna's Quest for Knowledge
beats.summary: Indradyumna, a sage who remembers his past life as a king, is instructed by the goddess Maya (who reveals herself as non-different from Vishnu) to worship Vishnu through knowledge and karma yoga. After her disappearance, he meditates and Vishnu appears, receiving his praise.

beats.title: Indradyumna's Vision and Vishnu's Teaching
beats.summary: The chapter begins with a series of hymns to Vishnu, followed by the sage Indradyumna praising the Lord. Vishnu appears, touches the sage, and grants him knowledge. Vishnu then teaches about the three types of bhāvanā (devotion) and the path to liberation through devotion to Ishvara.

beats.title: Indradyumna's Quest for Supreme Knowledge
beats.summary: Indradyumna asks Lord Kūrma about the supreme reality, his vibhūti, the nature of creation and dissolution, the varṇāśrama duties, and various cosmological details. Lord Kūrma answers, then disappears. Indradyumna, following the instruction, renounces everything and attains supreme yoga. Later, as a yogīndra, he travels to Mānasa-uttara mountain to see Brahmā, entering a luminous abode and perceiving the supreme being.

beats.title: The Kurma Purana: Origin and Teaching
beats.summary: The chapter describes the beginning of the Kurma Purana, where the sage Suta narrates the story of Lord Vishnu in his Kurma (tortoise) avatar. It recounts how the sage Indradyumna received spiritual knowledge from Vishnu, and how the sages led by Narada and Indra asked Vishnu to teach them. Vishnu then explains his own origin as Narayana, the creation of Brahma from his lotus, and the birth of Rudra from his anger. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of hearing this Purana.

beats.title: Creation of Rudra, Lakshmi, and the World Order
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of Rudra (Shiva) and Lakshmi from the primordial being, followed by Brahma's creation of the nine mind-born sons (Prajapatis) and the four varnas. It emphasizes the supremacy of the Vedas and warns against non-Vedic scriptures. The goddess Lakshmi is instructed to delude most beings but avoid those devoted to dharma and Ishvara.

beats.title: The Origin and Structure of Dharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana discusses the origin of dharma, the classification of varnas and ashramas, and the duties associated with each. It emphasizes the supremacy of the householder stage and the importance of following dharma as the means to attain artha, kama, and moksha.

beats.title: The Supremacy of Dharma and the Paths to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana discusses the supremacy of dharma (righteousness) as the foundation for artha (wealth), kama (desire), and moksha (liberation). It describes the three gunas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their corresponding destinations, the importance of combining karma yoga with jnana, the distinction between pravritta (worldly) and nivritta (renunciate) paths, and the characteristics of dharma for different varnas and ashramas. The sages ask about the four ashramas, and Lord Kurma explains that the true sannyasi is the one who renounces all actions and remains steady in yoga.

beats.title: The Four Ashramas and the Threefold Path
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the four ashramas (stages of life) and the threefold path of renunciation, yoga, and devotion. It explains the three forms of the Supreme Being (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva) as manifestations of the same ultimate reality, and emphasizes the importance of worshiping them with devotion throughout life.

beats.title: The Order of Ashramas and Marks of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana discusses the rules and procedures for the four ashramas (stages of life) — brahmacharya, grihastha, vanaprastha, and sannyasa. It emphasizes that renunciation (sannyasa) should be undertaken only after attaining true detachment (vairagya), and that one may enter sannyasa from any ashrama if properly qualified. The chapter also describes the wearing of tripundra (three horizontal lines of ash) and tilaka (mark) on the forehead as symbols of devotion to Shiva, Vishnu, or Brahma, linking these marks to the three gunas and the three deities.

beats.title: The Discourse on Karma Yoga and Creation
beats.summary: The chapter discusses the philosophy of karma yoga, emphasizing that actions performed without attachment and dedicated to the Supreme lead to liberation. It describes the four ashramas and the nature of the ultimate reality, with the sage Suta narrating the dialogue between the sages and Lord Kurma (Narayana).

beats.title: Cosmic Creation from the Unmanifest
beats.summary: This chapter describes the process of cosmic creation from the unmanifest Brahman through the interaction of Prakriti and Purusha, leading to the emergence of Mahat, Ahamkara, the five elements, and the sense organs. It explains the sequential evolution of the universe according to Samkhya cosmology.

beats.title: Cosmic Egg and the Threefold Manifestation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of the cosmic egg (brahmāṇḍa) from the five elements, the emergence of Brahmā as the first embodied being, and the threefold manifestation of the Supreme Lord as Brahmā (rajas), Viṣṇu (sattva), and Rudra (tamas) for creation, preservation, and dissolution. It also details the layers covering the egg and the beings residing in the higher realms.

beats.title: The Supreme Being and Cosmic Time
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the nature of the supreme being, who manifests in various forms for the welfare of devotees, and explains the cosmic time cycles including yugas and manvantaras.

beats.title: Cosmic Cycles and the Varaha Avatar
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the cosmic cycles of time, including the fourteen Manvantaras within a day of Brahma, the nature of Kalpas, and the dissolution of all beings into Prakriti at the end of Brahma's lifespan. It then narrates the beginning of the current Varaha Kalpa, where the universe was a single dark ocean, and Brahma (Narayana) slept on the waters. At the end of the night, he assumed the form of a boar (Varaha) to lift the earth from the depths of Rasatala, praised by the sages.

beats.title: Hymns to Vishnu and the Primary Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana contains hymns of praise to the supreme being (Vishnu) in various forms, followed by a description of the primary creation (sarga) by the creator. It details the emergence of fivefold ignorance (avidya) and the successive creation of immovable beings, animals, gods, and humans.

beats.title: The Nine Creations and the Birth of Rudra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the nine types of creation (sarga) according to Puranic cosmology, including the primary creation from the unmanifest, the creation of elements, senses, immovables, animals, humans, and the divine. It also narrates the birth of Rudra from Brahma's anger and the subsequent creation of various beings.

beats.title: Creation of Beings from Brahma's Body and Gunas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of various beings and entities by Brahma, including sages, gods, demons, humans, and other creatures, through different parts of his body and the guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas). It also explains the origin of day, night, twilight, and the sandhyā periods.

beats.title: Creation of the Vedas and the First Humans
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the creation of the Vedas, meters, and sacrificial forms from Brahma's mouths, followed by the creation of various beings. It then narrates Brahma's distress when his creations do not multiply, leading him to dispel darkness and create the first male and female, Manu and Shatarupa, who become the progenitors of humanity.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 21)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the creation of beings from Dharma and Adharma. It lists the daughters of Daksha and their marriages to Dharma and various sages, detailing their offspring. It also describes the progeny of Adharma, leading to negative entities. The chapter ends with the sages questioning Vishnu about Shiva's birth from Brahma.

beats.title: The Cosmic Play of Narayana and Brahma
beats.summary: The chapter describes the cosmic sleep of Narayana on the serpent Shesha in the primeval ocean, the emergence of a lotus from his navel, and the appearance of Brahma. A dialogue ensues where each claims supremacy, leading to a mutual display of cosmic power: Vishnu enters Brahma's body and shows the universe within, then Brahma enters Vishnu's body and sees the same worlds. The chapter ends with Brahma finding only the navel as an exit, highlighting the infinite nature of Vishnu.

beats.title: The Emergence of Brahma and the Revelation of the Supreme
beats.summary: Brahma emerges from a lotus from Vishnu's navel and claims supremacy. Vishnu corrects him, revealing that both are aspects of the same supreme reality, and directs him to seek refuge in Shiva, who appears as the supreme lord.

beats.title: Shiva Reveals His Supremacy to Brahma and Vishnu
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Kurma Purana, Lord Shiva appears before Brahma and Vishnu in a blazing form. Brahma, initially confused, is enlightened by Vishnu about Shiva's supreme nature. Brahma then praises Shiva and asks for a son like him. Shiva grants the boon, promising divine knowledge will arise.

beats.title: Shiva's Boon and the Slaying of Madhu and Kaitabha
beats.summary: Shiva (Maheshvara) grants a boon to Brahma, declaring that Vishnu (Narayana) is his own supreme form and that they are non-different. Vishnu asks for unwavering devotion to Shiva. Shiva then departs to his abode. Later, the demons Madhu and Kaitabha appear from Vishnu's ear-wax. At Brahma's request, Vishnu commands two beings to kill them. After the battle, Vishnu tells Brahma to descend from the lotus, as he cannot bear his tejas. Brahma then enters Vishnu's body and induces yogic sleep.

beats.title: The Origin of Rudra and His Eight Forms
beats.summary: Brahma, after being reminded by Vishnu, attempts creation but fails. In anger, he sheds tears that become ghosts and demons. He then gives up his life, and from his mouth emerges Rudra (Shiva), who is named Rudra because of his crying. Brahma grants Rudra seven names, eight forms, wives, and sons. Rudra then attains liberation by meditating on the supreme Brahman.

beats.title: Shiva's Creation of Rudras and Brahma's Hymn
beats.summary: Brahma praises Shiva as the supreme reality, describing his attributes and cosmic role. The chapter recounts Shiva's creation of Rudras, Brahma's request for mortal beings, and Shiva's subsequent stillness, becoming Sthanu. Brahma's hymn extols Shiva as the ultimate cause, pervading all existence.

beats.title: Brahma's Praise of Shiva and the Revelation of Trimurti
beats.summary: Brahma praises Mahadeva (Shiva) as the supreme reality manifesting as various cosmic forms and principles. Shiva then grants Brahma divine yoga, lordship, knowledge of Brahman, and detachment. He reveals that he has divided himself into Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva for creation, preservation, and dissolution, and that Brahma was born from his right side, Vishnu from his left, and Rudra from his heart. The chapter emphasizes the non-dual nature of the supreme being.

beats.title: Creation of Rudra and the Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the creation of Rudra and the goddess from Brahma's tapas, the division of Rudra into eleven Rudras and the goddess into various forms, the marriage of Rudra to Sati (later Parvati), and the birth of the nine Brahmans (Prajapatis) from Narayana. It also includes a dialogue between Brahma and Rudra about the supreme form of Shiva.

beats.title: The Supreme Goddess and the Powers of Shiva
beats.summary: The chapter describes the supreme goddess as the consort of Shiva, her various forms and powers, and the nature of time as a manifestation of the divine. It explains the four shaktis (Shanti, Vidya, Pratishtha, Nivritti) and the concept of Maya as the creative power of Ishvara.

beats.title: The Nature of Shakti and the Birth of Parvati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana discusses the nature of Shakti (divine energy) and its relationship with Shiva, the supreme consciousness. It describes Maya as the creative power of Shiva, the unity of Shakti and Shaktiman, and the path to liberation through devotion to Parvati. The story includes the birth of Parvati to Himavan and Mena, and their recognition of her divine nature.

beats.title: Himavan's Vision of the Goddess
beats.summary: Himavan, the king of mountains, encounters the Goddess (Parameshvari) and asks her identity. She reveals herself as the supreme Shakti, the essence of all beings, and grants him divine vision to see her cosmic form. Himavan then praises her with a hymn of 108 names, describing her as the ultimate reality, beyond all attributes, and the source of all powers.

beats.title: Hymn to the Supreme Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana (part 33) is a hymn describing the supreme goddess (Devi) as the primordial energy (Mula Prakriti), the source of all creation, preservation, and destruction. She is identified with various forms such as Mahamaya, Yogini, Brahmi, Lakshmi, Sarasvati, Parvati, and others, and is the consort of Shiva, Vishnu, and Brahma. The text emphasizes her transcendence, immanence, and role as the ultimate reality.

beats.title: Hymn to the Goddess as Sarasvati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana is a hymn listing the many names and attributes of the goddess, identified as Sarasvati, Durga, and the supreme divine feminine. It describes her as the source of all knowledge, power, and creation, and as the consort of Vishnu (Vasudeva). The text emphasizes her manifold forms and her role in granting boons, destroying demons, and being the ultimate reality.

beats.title: Hymn to the Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana is a hymn listing numerous names and epithets of the Goddess (Devi), describing her as the supreme power, the source of all, and the embodiment of various energies and attributes. It emphasizes her role in creation, preservation, and destruction, and her presence in all beings.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 36)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana (part 36) is a hymn listing hundreds of names and epithets of the Goddess (Devi), describing her various forms, attributes, and associations. It includes references to her as Durgā, Kātyāyanī, Caṇḍī, Bhadrakālī, and many others, highlighting her role as the supreme mother, destroyer of demons, and bestower of boons.

beats.title: The Thousand Names of the Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana lists numerous names and epithets of the Goddess, describing her various forms, attributes, and associations. It emphasizes her role as the supreme deity, creator of daityas and danavas, and source of all powers.

beats.title: Himavan's Hymn to the Goddess
beats.summary: Himavan praises the Goddess with a thousand names, describing her as the supreme power, the source of all creation, and the consort of Shiva. Terrified by her fierce form, he requests a milder one. She obliges, appearing as a beautiful, serene goddess. Himavan then extols her as the ultimate reality, Prakriti, and the support of all existence.

beats.title: Hymn to the Supreme Goddess
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to the supreme goddess, identifying her with various deities, cosmic principles, and sacred elements. It describes her as the ultimate reality, beyond attributes, and the source of all creation.

beats.title: Hymn to the Cosmic Forms and the Goddess's Instruction
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Kurma Purana, the sage or devotee offers a series of hymns to the supreme deity, describing various cosmic forms including the cosmic egg, the sun, Narayana, the destructive form of time, Shesha, Rudra, and the goddess. The chapter culminates with the goddess Parvati (Girija) instructing her father Himavan on a secret teaching about the supreme reality, emphasizing the need for a calm, concentrated mind free from pride and ego.

beats.title: The Supremacy of the Vedas and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana presents a discourse on the supremacy of the Vedas, the importance of dharma, and the path to liberation through devotion, knowledge, and action. It emphasizes that the Vedas are the ultimate source of dharma, and that practices like the Pancharatra and Kapala are misleading. The chapter also describes the role of sages like Vyasa in preserving dharma through the Puranas and other scriptures.

beats.title: Discourse on Liberation through Devotion and Knowledge
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana (part 42) presents a discourse on the path to liberation through devotion, knowledge, and surrender to the supreme being (Shiva). It emphasizes the importance of dharma, jnana, and bhakti, describing the characteristics of devotees who attain the supreme state. The text contrasts the perishable world with the eternal, attributeless Brahman, and advises the seeker to worship the formless or manifest aspects of the divine according to their capacity.

beats.title: The Goddess's Instruction to Himavan and the Marriage of Parvati
beats.summary: The chapter describes the dialogue between the goddess (Devi) and the mountain king Himavan, where she reveals her divine origin and instructs him on the path to liberation through devotion to Shiva. It culminates in Himavan giving his daughter Parvati in marriage to Shiva, and concludes with the merits of reciting the hymn to the Devi.

beats.title: Genealogy of Sages and Fires
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the lineage of various sages and deities, including the birth of Lakshmi from Bhrigu and Khyati, the progeny of Marichi, Pulaha, Atri, Angiras, Pulastya, Kratu, and Vasistha, and the origin of the forty-nine fires from Rudra-identified Agni and Svaha.

beats.title: Lineage of Svayambhuva Manu and the Birth of Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the lineage of Svayambhuva Manu, focusing on the descendants of Priyavrata and Uttanapada, including Dhruva, and the birth of Prithu from Vena. It also recounts the origin of the Suta tradition and the appearance of Hari as a Suta to narrate Puranas.

beats.title: The Initiation of King Suśīla by Sage Śvetāśvatara
beats.summary: King Vainya (also called Vena) performs a sacrifice and receives a boon from Lord Vishnu that his sons will be righteous. His son Shikhandana (also named Suśīla) becomes a learned and devout king. He goes to the Himalayas, bathes in the Mandākinī river, worships Śiva, and meets the sage Śvetāśvatara, who initiates him into the sannyāsa order and imparts the knowledge of Paśupata yoga, emphasizing meditation on the formless Śiva.

beats.title: The Lineage of Prācīnabarhi and the Curse of Dakṣa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Kurma Purana describes the lineage of Prācīnabarhi and the Pracetas, the birth of Dakṣa, and the conflict between Dakṣa and Rudra (Śiva) leading to Satī's self-immolation and Rudra's curse. It also recounts the instruction of King Suśīla by a sage, leading him to become a Pāśupata ascetic.

beats.title: Dakṣa's Sacrifice and the Insult to Śiva
beats.summary: The sages of Naimiṣāraṇya ask Sūta to narrate the creation of gods, demons, and other beings in the Vaivasvata Manvantara. Sūta recounts how Dakṣa, cursed by Śiva, performed a sacrifice at Gaṅgādvāra without inviting Śiva. The sage Dadhīca rebukes Dakṣa for not worshipping Rudra, who is the supreme lord and identical with the sun. Dakṣa argues that Rudra is not entitled to a share in sacrifices. The assembled sages, deluded by Viṣṇu's māyā, side with Dakṣa and insult Śiva. Only Nārāyaṇa (Viṣṇu) perceives the true nature of Śiva.

beats.title: Destruction of Dakṣa's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Dadhīca curses the Brahmins who assisted Dakṣa's sacrifice for disrespecting Śiva, predicting their downfall in Kali Yuga. Satī informs Śiva, who creates Vīrabhadra and Bhadrakālī to destroy the sacrifice.

beats.title: Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Rudra and his ganas, led by Virabhadra, disrupt Daksha's sacrifice after being denied a share. They destroy the yajna, attack the gods, and are eventually pacified by Brahma.

beats.title: The Reconciliation of Daksha and Shiva
beats.summary: After the destruction of Daksha's sacrifice by Virabhadra, the gods and Daksha seek refuge in Shiva. Shiva appears with Parvati, forgives Daksha, and instructs him to become a devotee. Brahma then teaches Daksha about the non-duality of Vishnu and Shiva, emphasizing that they are one and the same, and that worshiping either leads to liberation.

beats.title: Daksha's Creation and the Genealogy of Gods
beats.summary: Daksha, commanded by Brahma to create beings, first creates gods, gandharvas, sages, asuras, and nagas. When his creations do not multiply, he resorts to procreation through marriage. He marries Asikni, daughter of Viraṇa, and begets a thousand sons, but they are destroyed by Narada's maya. He then creates sixty daughters with Vairaṇī. He gives ten daughters to Dharma, thirteen to Kashyapa, twenty-seven to Soma, four to Ariṣṭanemi, two to Bahuputra, two to Kṛśāśva, and two to Aṅgiras. The offspring of these unions are described, including the Vasus, Viśvadevas, Sādhyas, Maruts, etc. The chapter also recounts the story of Daksha's yajna and the curse of Dadhīca's sages, who later attain liberation through devotion to Shiva.

beats.title: The Birth of Hiranyakashipu and Vishnu's Response
beats.summary: The chapter describes the origin of the Adityas (twelve sun gods) from Aditi and Kashyapa, the birth of the demons Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha from Diti and Kashyapa, Hiranyakashipu's boon from Brahma, his oppression of the gods, the gods' plea to Vishnu, and Vishnu's creation of a being (Narasimha) to kill Hiranyakashipu.
